data.title: Exelon: Not Just A Bet On AI Data Center Tailwinds, But On Electrification Overall
data.description:
Introduction

Exelon Corporation (EXC) just reported its Q4 and full-year results that pleased investors, as the company’s outlook is fueled by data center tailwinds and a massive increase in capex for the next few years to support AI data center infrastructure.

By The Numbers

Sales came in at $5.41B, down around 1% y/y, and missed consensus by $80m. Three out of the four revenue segments showed quite good performance, growing between 14.5% to 23.8%, while ComEd saw its revenues decline by around 40%, which explains the lackluster overall top-line performance. The management explained this 40% drop anomaly and said that it was largely a “pass-through accounting shift.” The reason for this is that wholesale energy prices dropped significantly compared to where they were just a year ago, so the segment’s revenues plummeted; however, since the company is not allowed to make a profit on the energy itself, the segment actually improved operationally because its operating income increased.

Speaking of operating profit, ComEd saw its adjusted operating income increase to $252m from $243m, so as we can see, the 40% decline in revenue is not affecting the company’s profitability. PECO’s adjusted operating earnings decreased to $162m from $196m, BGE’s to $181m from $175m, and PHI’s to $171m from $132m. PECO’s lackluster performance was attributed to higher taxes and contracting costs, while the other ones saw their profits increasing due to data center demand and “multi-year plan” rate reconciliations. Overall, the company’s adjusted EPS for the quarter came in at $0.59, beating estimates by 6 cents, while for the full year, EPS was $2.77 vs. $2.50 last year.

The company’s financial position at the end of the year was quite good, with over $626m in cash, against $47B in long-term debt, which doesn’t look very good, but it is quite normal to have such high amounts of debt for companies like EXC. Their business is very capital-intensive, so it is expected that the debt amount is very high. It is more of a strategic tool than anything else. The company’s utilities are essentially legal monopolies, which means they can set rates that will be able to cover the interest they pay on their debt, and since everyone needs electricity, the cash flow is highly predictable. Banks are much more willing to lend to EXC than to a startup. Speaking of cash flows, the CFO came in at $6.25B, while FCF came in at -$2.275B due to high capital expenditures for the year, which totaled $8.5B and a notable increase from last year’s $7B. So, I am not worried about the debt pile; it’s just another day at the office for the company.

On top of these results, the management gave us guidance for the full year ahead. Adjusted EPS will be in the range of $2.81-$2.91, which is around 6% growth y/y, while the consensus expected it to be $2.83 a share.

It seems that investors like the report, as the company’s share price increased on the day and has continued to rise ever since. The company’s share price is up around 10% since the report. The investors looked past the revenue miss and rewarded the company’s decoupling efforts from volatile energy prices. The company is becoming a lot more like an infrastructure play, which is much more predictable and less risky than if the company remained a power generator.

Comments On The Outlook

The insatiable hunger for power from hyperscalers is benefiting many companies, and there is no doubt that the companies that allow hyperscalers to achieve their AI dreams, like the energy companies and anything adjacent to energy, like infrastructure, will continue to reap profits going forward. The only growth industry at the moment is the data center and AI business. So, it is not a surprise that Exelon is looking to take advantage of this massive demand for data centers. The company announced $41B in capex spending through 2029 to support demand for power from new AI data centers, upgrade the grid so that it could withstand volatile weather patterns better, and integrate renewable energy resources, like wind and solar, into its existing distribution network.

I cannot blame the company for chasing data center money. It’s a no-brainer. A lot of the planned capex will go towards upgrading the grid to handle the massive, concentrated AI data center requirements. This is not a surprise. Hyperscalers massively increased their capex for 2026, so it is only natural to see that companies that are there to support the AI dreams are also looking to spend billions more as well. The pipeline includes 19 GW for data center projects, which is a ridiculous amount, to put it lightly. This amount of energy can power over 16m homes if we take this estimate at face value, which says that 1GW could power 876K households for one year. And all of that is going to some data centers instead.

So, the shiny new object of data centers is a massive growth factor for many companies, but besides that, EXC has other growth opportunities. Opportunities that may not be as lucrative as data centers but are still good enough to provide growth. Electric vehicle adoption will help increase a home’s electricity consumption by quite a bit. Some reports suggest up to a 50% increase in electricity consumption. Exelon is looking to spend heavily on managed charging infrastructure so that as adoption increases, people will be able to charge their cars safely without blowing any of the transformers.

Another positive growth comes from the increased demand for heat pumps. There is a big push in some states in the U.S. to switch from gas furnaces to heat pumps. So, this push can potentially shift massive amounts of energy demand onto the electric grid, especially during the winter peaks. So, upgrading the grid is a very good idea for the company, which will pay dividends in the long run.

Should You Invest In EXC?

I think the company is a good choice, not only because of the AI data center tailwinds for the next couple of years, but also because I believe the company’s future is certain. The global digitization and electrification efforts to get away from fossil fuels and into alternatives are going to continue to increase, and as the infrastructure builder of the future, the company is exposed to many different alternative power investments around the world.

I think you could view EXC as the pick and shovel seller because it is taking advantage of the AI gold rush by accommodating hyperscaler demand, and also you can look at the company as a pure play on electrification, since the world will continue to evolve, and that means a lot of power is going to be needed, and that power will have to go through EXC’s wires, no matter how the energy is being made, whether that is alternative fuels or fossil fuels.

So, if you would like more AI exposure or a pure electrification play, EXC could be a good choice as a long-term investment.

data.title: RQI: My Favorite And 8.4% Yielding Way To Invest In REITs Now
data.description:
Back in April, 2025, I wrote a bullish piece on Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), where I had identified several catalysts that could propel both the monthly dividend and the share price higher from then-current level.

I have to say that so far my thesis has been playing out very well:

RQI has delivered strong total returns, outperformed the REIT index (VNQ), and announced a juicy dividend bump of ~12%.

Because of this dividend (or distribution) increase, RQI's forward yield now stands at an attractive level of 8.4%, which, without the registered increase, would have been much lower due to the notable share price growth.

In fact, we can even observe that RQI's yield has become more attractive than before (e.g., it has increased by ~14% over the past 12 months):

In the meantime, REIT space or VNQ has experienced the opposite effect with its compressing yields, which is completely logical given how the interest rate environment has evolved.

In this context, some might argue that there is probably a justified reason behind RQI's yield divergence from the rest of the REIT world and that, arguably, these distributions are unsustainable or, to some extent, funded by a return-of-capital component, which is not that uncommon in the high-yield CEF space.

Well, I have reassessed the investment case and arrived at the conclusion that RQI remains a very strong buy.

Let me explain.

Thesis Review

One of the key drivers behind my interest in leveraged CEF instruments that are biased towards REITs is attributable to the suboptimal REIT growth (return) prospects.

Namely, the prevailing macro dynamics do not bode well for strong REIT returns that can be enabled by falling long-term yields. Instead, such aspects as stubbornly high inflation, a weakening dollar, and deglobalization clearly support elevated long-term yield levels. We can easily observe this by looking at the yield curve's reaction function to the previous base rate cuts. While the front end of the curve has moved down, the 7+ year segment has remained largely unchanged.

All of this has also been reflected in relatively stagnant REIT/VNQ returns. So, the risk is that by opening pure-play REIT positions, we are likely to experience no meaningful price returns and, what is important to understand, no tangible yield either - e.g., currently, VNQ yields ~3.7%, which on an inflation-adjusted basis is clearly unattractive.

Now, this is where RQI comes into play.

The overall idea of RQI is to take long positions in high-quality REITs and add an external leverage overlay to enhance the underlying core yield. This solves the unattractive yield issue and introduces a more enticing upside potential in case of a sector-wide recovery (valuation multiple expansion).

Granted, the presence of leverage introduces the opposite risk as well, but given how cheap the overall REIT space is and the fact that the capital has started to shift from growth into value, I do not see downside risk as a high-probability event. Plus, let's also remember that RQI portfolio primarily consists of well-capitalized, large-cap investment grade REITs, which help de-risk the situation:

On top of that, about 18% of RQI's holdings are tied to fixed income and/or preferred securities that are issued by the same (mostly) investment-grade REITs.

Speaking about the recently increased dividend and its sustainability, there are two items that are worth appreciating.

First, as of now, RQI has ~30% of its asset base fueled by external leverage, which has become an even bigger advantage as the probabilities of incremental base rate cuts this year have gone up. The lower the base rates, the cheaper it gets for RQI to service the debt, which, in turn, provides a direct positive support to the dividend coverage.

Second, the dividend coverage itself has been very robust, meaning that RQI has not tapped into its NAV base in order to keep the distributions running. Instead, there is a balance between net interest income generation and realization of long-term capital gains that supports RQI's distributions.

In fact, in December 2025, RQI was even able to distribute an extraordinary (supplemental) dividend of $0.13 per unit on top of the regular one without utilizing any ROC proceeds. And this happened right before the ~12% bump was made for the regular monthly dividend, which is a clear testament to RQI's ability to accommodate the current yield in a sustainable fashion.

The Bottom Line

The recent distribution hike has been fully justified, going well in line with the underlying cash flows and financial dynamics. The dividend coverage statistics (NII and long-term capital gain heavy) confirm that this is indeed the case, where the most important thing to appreciate is the fact that no ROC proceeds have been involved in the distribution process.

On a go forward basis, RQI should be able to sustain the current ~8.4% yield in exactly the same, sustainable manner, where such aspects as investment grade bias and likely lower base rates should come in handy.

The only thing that can materially derail RQI from the current track is a sudden plunge across the entire REIT market, which in that case would neutralize the long-term gain component that is currently playing a meaningful role in the overall dividend equation.

However, I view such a risk as a low probability event given how relatively depressed the REIT prices are and considering that the lion's share of the RQI portfolio is tied to either IG corporates or fixed income/preferred share securities. On top of that, let's also remember that a falling base rate environment should provide a tailwind for RQI on its NII per unit generation front, increasing the role of the NII component for facilitating the dividend payments.

In a nutshell, I remain bullish on RQI.

data.title: Crocs: Cleaning Up Wholesale, Share Repurchases At A Deeply Discounted Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

Crocs (CROX) closed out its fourth quarter with flattish revenues, with both the top and bottom lines above expectations thanks to better inventory management, direct-to-consumer (DTC) growth, and margin stabilization. The core Crocs brand showed stability, especially outside the U.S., while the HEYDUDE brand continued its turnaround with wholesale cleanup and selective promotional strategies. With strong free cash flow and aggressive share repurchases, at a valuation that’s below 7x forward earnings, I think Crocs is a buy.

A Look at the Most Recent Quarter

Crocs closed out 2025 with strong results that beat on both the top and bottom lines. In Q4, revenues clocked in at $958 million, down 4%, but that was an improvement sequentially from Q3. The Crocs brand generated $768 million, driven by 11% international growth. While this was partially offset by an 18% decline to $189 million at HEYDUDE, margins held up relatively well, which allowed for a 38-cent beat on EPS to $2.29 in the quarter.

When looking at the full-year results, FY’25 revenues were just over $4 billion, down 1.5% compared to last year as the company navigated some volatility across its brands. At Crocs, which accounts for 82% of total company revenue, full-year revenues were up only 1%, with the DTC business climbing 3% and wholesale channels dipping 1%. The reason for this was deliberate efforts to manage promotions and inventory flow.

In terms of the economics, units sold for the Crocs brand climbed 2% to 129 million pairs, with average selling prices holding roughly flat. Regionally, North America saw a 7% drop to $1.7 billion, driven by reduced DTC promotions and controlled wholesale sell-in. International revenues climbed 11% to $1.6 billion thanks to 23% DTC growth and 5% wholesale growth. In the international business, the big standout was China's sales growth, up 30% compared to last year.

For the HEYDUDE brand (the boat-shoe-style line they bought a few years ago), which accounts for the remaining 18% of total company sales, revenues were down 14% compared to last year as DTC edged up 3% because of new store openings and digital strength. Offsetting this was wholesale, which fell 27% due to channel cleanup and moderated performance marketing. HEYDUDE's average selling prices rose 4% to just under $32, but units declined 17% to 22 million pairs.

While negative growth across both wholesale channels might be disappointing at first glance, I actually believe these are the right steps for the company over the longer term. In its largest market in North America, the Crocs brand has deliberately slowed down how much product they ship to wholesale partners by avoiding overstocking, keeping prices stable, and protecting the brand from being discounted and being perceived as cheap. This essentially meant fewer wholesale sales compared to last year as the company focused more on selling to customers online or in their own stores (which grew this year).

Margins Are Stabilizing

At HEYDUDE, the actions were similar in that the company took back excess inventory from retailers and gave allowances for returns or markdowns. They also reduced heavy advertising spending that wasn't working well. Rather than pushing volume at any cost, the strategy around the wholesale business is to fix messy inventory piles in stores. That’ll most likely improve how the brand looks on shelves and set up a healthier, more profitable base for future sales.

In the meantime, though, margins have taken a small hit. For example, total company gross margins for the full year were 50 bps lower than last year at 58.3%, and while that included a 130 bps impact from tariffs, lower sourcing costs did help to offset this. The Crocs brand's gross margin was 61.3%, slipping 30 bps, while HEYDUDE's stood at 44.8%, off 290 bps (again, more work had to be done in wholesale). The margin compression was also felt further down the income statement as SG&A was 7% higher compared to last year, and operating margins of 22.3% were 330 bps lower than last year.

In terms of key highlights from the quarter and year, Crocs had a strong holiday season and had good customer reception to new clog innovations like the Unfurgettable Clog and extensions in the Echo and Baya franchises, which reinforced the brand’s leadership in clogs (still 74% of the mix). On the earnings call, management noted that Sandals approached $450 million in sales, representing 13% of the portfolio and capturing notable market share gains in North America with an extended selling season. Jibbitz and personalization accessories held steady at 8% of revenues, driven by elevated charms and new bag collections. With an ongoing NFL deal, a sold-out Stranger Things collaboration, a high-resale Classic Twilight clog, and the newly announced global LEGO starting early this year, there’s a number of partnerships that should help drive sales and amplify engagement.

With a still-strong free cash flow profile and a deeply discounted share price, management has been using its cash flow to buy back stock. With FCF generation at $660 million, $128 million was spent on debt reduction, and $577 million was spent on buybacks. With buybacks equaling roughly 10% of shares outstanding, the company has quickly become a true “share cannibal.” This hasn’t come at the expense of the balance sheet, however, as the company had $130 million in cash with over $900 million in revolver capacity. With another $747 million remaining on the repurchase authorization and leverage at 1.2x, I’d expect continued buybacks at current prices.

An Attractive Setup for 2026

As for the outlook, management took the Q4 call to provide guidance for 2026. For this year, the company expects revenues to range from down 1% to up slightly, with the Crocs brand expected to hold flat to up 2%, with 10% international growth that offsets anticipated North America softness as the company laps those 2H'25 actions like promo pullbacks and inventory management.

For HEYDUDE, the projection of a 7% to 9% decline feels pragmatic, but I believe it underpins a turnaround narrative with a return to growth in the second half, as those cleanup efforts fade and new products like Stretch Jersey gain traction.

Overall, this guidance suggests a year of stabilization rather than aggressive expansion, but with adjusted operating margins modestly expanding from 22.3% and EPS in the $12.88 to $13.35 range, it tells me the focus on cost efficiencies and buybacks would yield upside if demand is steady against tariffs.

Short-term, the first quarter looks like a continuation of recent headwinds, with revenues guided down 3.5-5.5%, Crocs dipping low single digits led by international, and HEYDUDE facing a steeper 15% to 18% drop. That to me implies a back-loaded year, with second-half revenue growth outpacing the first half, potentially turning positive as North America Crocs sees slight run-rate improvements and HEYDUDE laps easier comps. By prioritizing channel health over forced sales, the business will be in a better spot for much easier comps come 2027.

Longer-term, I think there’s good growth drivers with international expansion, where markets like China, Japan, and India offer multi-year runways with current shares at about a third of established levels. In my view, with a $100 million cost savings initiative, split between COGS and SG&A, I think Crocs’ management has enough firepower for investments in AI and other cost efficiencies. With capex modest at $70-80 million, higher than the $51.2 million in FY'25, this is still a figure less than 2% of total company revenues.

Risks and Valuation

In my view, the primary risk to the investment thesis for Crocs centers on the persistent softness in North America for the core Crocs brand, which accounted for a 7% revenue decline in 2025 and is expected to face ongoing headwinds this year. How this geography performs is pretty critical to the underlying business results, and if demand doesn't stabilize in a spending environment where lower-end consumers stay cautious, it could drag overall growth and delay a return to positive growth.

While there are tariff concerns (estimated at an $80 million impact this year) and concerns that competition could always put pressure on margins, I think the valuation compensates you for much of the risk here. At 6.5x EV/EBITDA and 7.9x P/E, the company trades at half the multiple of peers at 11.1x EV/EBITDA and 17.1x P/E (source: Bloomberg). Even against its own valuation, Crocs looks inexpensive, trading right in line with its own historical EV/EBITDA valuation range.

Share Repurchases Are a Catalyst

What I like about the Crocs thesis is that you don’t really need the Crocs brand to grow in order for the stock to work. As long as the company continues trading around this level below 10x earnings, management will likely continue buying back 10% of shares year in and year out. And if we do indeed have a business here that’s stabilizing, EPS can likely continue growing at a high-single-digit CAGR just because of share repurchases alone. Any growth that might come out of a turn in North American sales or continued international growth would just be the cherry on top.

With sell-side estimates where they are, you can see how quickly EPS could grow and how pathetic the valuation looks at these levels. At 6.9x forward earnings today, Crocs is likely trading at a sub-6x earnings multiple just two years out as a result of buybacks.

Bottom Line

Crocs is a story of patience that I think will pay off. The company’s careful approach to managing inventory, controlling wholesale, and focusing on direct-to-consumer sales may have slowed revenue growth in the short term, but I think it strengthens the foundation for long-term profitability. Coupled with robust free cash flow and aggressive share repurchases, there’s a natural catalyst for earnings growth and realizing value even if the top-line growth is modest.

Going forward, I expect that upside to come from international markets, product innovations, and ongoing cost efficiencies. But even without a big surge in sales, the combination of a disciplined strategy and an undervalued stock makes Crocs a name I’m comfortable owning. And I think the market may be underestimating just how powerful that setup might be.

data.title: British American Tobacco 2025 Earnings: Battleground United States
data.description:
Introduction

Major tobacco company British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI, BTAFF) reported its results for 2025 yesterday. It is the third tobacco company in a row whose annual results I review here on Seeking Alpha, following my articles on Altria Group, Inc. (MO) and Philip Morris International Inc. (PM).

This update on British American Tobacco is quite a bit more important than my last two articles on MO and PM because I haven't reviewed the company in more than a year. Furthermore, in my last article—published in November of 2024—I focused more on the potential impact of donations from President Trump than on the company's operations.

In this article, I'll give an update on British American's cigarette portfolio—after all, it remains the core driver of free cash flow. Speaking of which, the company reported a 37% year-over-year decline in operating cash flow, which therefore warrants a close look in view of the dividend coverage and debt servicing capacity. Moreover, I'll share my take on BTI's smoke-free products portfolio, discussing both positive and negative developments in its key domains. When appropriate, I draw comparisons with British American's closest peer, Philip Morris International.

On a side note, I continue to review BTI starting with 2018, the first full-year after the acquisition of Reynolds American Inc.

British American Tobacco 2025 Results Review

Surprisingly Strong Performance In Modern Oral Overshadowed By Disappointments In E-Vapor And Heated Tobacco

For 2025, British American Tobacco delivered adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of £3.521, up 3.4% year-over-year, based on net revenues of £25.6 billion, down 1.0% year-over-year (Figure 1). The U.S. dollar weakness in 2025 was the main driver of the 3.1 percentage point currency-related headwind, so on a currency-neutral basis, BTI reported net revenue growth of 2.1%.

From a segment perspective, BTI's cigarette segment saw a volume decline of 7.9% year-over-year. I'd like to refer readers to my latest update on U.S.-focused peer Altria Group, in which I discuss several driving factors of the currently above-average volume decline rate.

In terms of cigarette market share, British American Tobacco reported an, in my view, negligible decline of 10 bps. I attribute this to an ongoing shift of smokers to discount brands.

BTI's combustibles portfolio is dominated by cigarette sales but contains a small contribution from sales of make-your-own cigarettes tobacco, as well as pipe tobacco and cigarillos. The segment as a whole generated net revenues of £20.2 billion in 2025, down 2.3% year-over-year (up 1.0% at constant rates). Quite clearly, despite a reasonably good performance in diversifying into smoke-free products, the company continues to generate the majority of its revenues from the sale of combustibles (78.9% in 2025 to be accurate).

Looking at BTI's smoke-free segment, I was encouraged by the extremely solid performance in its Modern Oral segment. The success is driven by the company's nicotine pouch franchise Velo, the currently most serious competitor to Philip Morris' ZYN brand (which it acquired via Swedish Match). According to management's regional analysis, Velo Plus was mainly responsible for the extremely solid expansion in category volume share by—believe it or not—11.6 percentage points in the U.S. Quite clearly, Velo is taking market share from ZYN, while Altria's on! continues to be squeezed between the two giants. As a result of Velo's continued strong performance, segment volume was up 47% globally year-over-year:

Net revenues grew in line with volumes, which was great to see as well (Figure 3). Of course, however, and to dampen investor optimism somewhat, the category still accounts for less than 5% of consolidated net revenues.

In contrast to Velo, BTI's e-vapor franchise centered around the Vuse and Vype brands continues to face pressure from illicit disposable vaping products, especially in the U.S. These are also cited by Altria's management as an important driving factor of the cross-category movement underlying the currently above-average (but recently stabilized) volume decline in combustibles.

2025 was the second year of decline, both from a volume (Figure 4) and net revenue perspective (Figure 5). This segment will likely be surpassed by the Modern Oral segment in a year or two from a net revenue perspective. However, the company continues to innovate, and the launch of Vuse Ultra (along with increased efforts to tackle illegal products) should hopefully help to revitalize the category.

Finally, British American's performance in heated tobacco (glo brand) was also far from reassuring. Volume share was down 1.5 percentage points, likely and largely due to the fierce competition from Philip Morris' in-house developed IQOS franchise. Net revenues were down 0.7% in 2025, or up 1.0% at constant exchange rates, against the backdrop of 20 billion sticks shipped in 2025 (down 3.7% year-over-year). Hopes in this segment now rest on the introduction of glo Hilo in BTI's core markets.

Let's now turn to British American's financial performance in 2025.

Margin-wise, operations continue to look solid, at least from a gross profitability standpoint (Figure 6, blue line). Conversely, free cash flow declined precipitously in 2025, resulting in an adjusted free cash flow margin of just 15% (down roughly 50% year-over-year, red line in Figure 6).

Before looking closer at free cash flow, I'd like to add that I adjusted BTI's numbers for working capital movements (three-year rolling average), stock-based compensation, lease payments, and dividends paid to non-controlling interests. As the company reports its interest payments under "cash flows from financing activities," they aren't included in operating cash flow. Of course, I also deducted net interest expenses in the process of calculating free cash flow.

So, what was responsible for what looks like a veritable cash flow vacuum?

Most importantly, British American paid an initial £2.6 billion payment as part of the so-called "Approved Plans," according to which subsidiaries of British American Tobacco, but also Philip Morris International Inc. and Japan Tobacco International (JAPAY, JAPAF) together are required to pay almost £18 billion over the next couple of years. Those interested in further details on the matter should take a look at p. 14 of the 2025 full-year earnings press release.

Tax payments, stemming from tax deferrals in the U.S. in 2024, reduced 2025 operating cash flow by £678 million, and the company also paid £479 in connection with the Franked Investment Income Group Litigation Order (see p. 278 et seq. of BTI's 2025 annual report).

While these three items will continue to impact the company's operating cash flow negatively going forward, they will do so in a well-manageable threshold and therefore do not put the coverage of the dividend at risk, in my view.

I remain confident in British American's financial stability and its ability to pay a (slowly) growing dividend also after having taken a fresh look at the company's balance sheet. Net debt declined once again in 2025 and is now down to almost £30 billion (Figure 7). I still remember well the concerns of investors in 2022, when the tobacco company's exposure to U.S. dollar-denominated debt was particularly scrutinized. Given the significantly improved exchange rate (from the perspective of BTI's reporting currency), its U.S. dollar-denominated debt is now far less of an issue.

Investors should also appreciate the fact that the company's long-term credit rating was upgraded from Baa2 to Baa1 back in August of 2024 by Moody's. In its periodic review from August 2025, the rating agency found it appropriate to maintain the Baa1 rating with a stable outlook.

This makes a lot of sense, in my view, not only considering British American's improved net debt but also given management's free cash flow guidance. In the earnings update, management reaffirmed the expectation to generate approximately £50 billion in free cash flow between 2024 and 2030. This translates to free cash flow of roughly £38 billion over the next five years, including 2026, or about £7.6 billion annually.

Factoring in the 2025 dividend payout of £5.2 billion, this translates to a forward payout ratio of just shy of 70%. The company announced a 2% (in sterling terms) dividend increase to £2.4504 per share on an annualized basis, up from £2.4024. In addition, management expects to repurchase £1.3 billion worth of shares in 2026, up £200 million from the 2025 level. £1.3 billion would represent a theoretical reduction in share count by 1.36%, based on today's closing price of £43.54 per share on the London Stock Exchange and 2.199 billion weighted average shares outstanding in 2025 on a diluted basis. By comparison, in 2025, British American's share count declined by 1.2% thanks to share buybacks.

Conclusion

British American Tobacco p.l.c., previously widely shunned by investors, has returned to favor, as evidenced by its strong share price recovery. The company's combustible products segment, which continues to be the largest contributor to free cash flow generation, remains robust but faces significantly stronger volume declines compared to its closest peer, Philip Morris International.

At the same time, British American Tobacco is doing an excellent job of developing its nicotine pouch segment into a significant competitor to Philip Morris' ZYN. In this context, the U.S. strikes me as a veritable "battleground." While ZYN continues to lead in the U.S., British American's market share expansion by 11.6 percentage points is extremely encouraging. At the same time, Philip Morris is not affected by the continuing above-average decline in cigarette volumes in the U.S. and benefits from its superior geographical and brand-related exposure. In the heated tobacco products segment, Philip Morris has already got its foot in the door in the promising U.S. market thanks to its agreement related to the marketing of IQOS with Altria Group.

If the rest of the world is any indication of the upcoming developments in the U.S., I wouldn't get my hopes up too high about British American's potential in the heated tobacco products market. At the same time, it's worth remembering that ZYN was long considered the undisputed market leader in oral nicotine products, with competitors unable to catch up. BTI's Velo franchise is definitely beginning to teach Philip Morris and previously skeptical investors a lesson.

I think it requires no further discussion why I remain a confident shareholder in British American Tobacco (and its peers). Compared to Philip Morris stock, British American Tobacco shares are still significantly cheaper, as indicated by the current price-earnings ratio of 12.4 (based on adjusted EPS for 2025). BTI shares are trading at a discount of around 50% to PM shares (valuation metrics can be found here), but that does not automatically mean that PM shares are a Sell and BTI shares are a Buy. In my view, PM deserves a significant premium, as I explain in detail in my coverage, but make no mistake: a P/E ratio of around 25 is certainly a bit lofty. Conversely, British American's efforts in the nicotine pouch products segment are, in my view, somewhat underestimated, while the below-average performance of Vuse and glo certainly justifies a discount, as does the comparatively weaker combustibles business.

I rate British American Tobacco stock a hold at this level. And while I feel somewhat more confident owning a large position in Philip Morris stock from an operational excellence perspective, I feel more confident as a British American Tobacco shareholder from a valuation standpoint.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Broadcom: Why AI Vibe-Coding Is Not A Threat To Its Infrastructure Software Segment
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) investors might have thought recent and very large cap-ex announcements by customers Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet/Google (GOOG) would have caused a rebound in what has been a pretty brutal and persistent sell-off that began after last quarter's earnings report. Then came reports that HBM (high bandwidth memory) prices have jumped and could stay higher for another year or two. Now, the latest threat to Broadcom appears to be the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative wherein AI vibe-coding will threaten its high-margin Infrastructure Software Segment.

Specifically, Anthropic (ANTHRO) has prioritized high-value AI coding tools, and the company's latest Claude plugins have seemingly been heat-seeking missiles to destroy vulnerable software companies across the sector. Indeed, over the past month, this narrative has erased hundreds of billions in the market-cap of companies like Microsoft (MSFT), ETFs like the iShares Expanded Tech Software ETF (IGV), and even Broadcom (see chart below). Today, I'll explain why this narrative is irrational as it pertains to Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment and why I am upgrading Broadcom to a Strong Buy.

Investment Thesis

My followers and Seeking Alpha investors in general are likely very familiar with the investment thesis supporting Broadcom: it's because Broadcom has what I have termed the "AI Trifecta":

  • A leading provider of cutting-edge high-speed networking equipment.
  • A leading provider of optimized XPU compute engines for AI data centers.
  • A leading provider of high-margin Infrastructure Software including VMware virtualization software that includes full-stack support for Nvidia GPUs, AMD GPUs, and – of course – Broadcom's XPUs, offering VMware customers tremendous value-added flexibility.

These business lines, combined with a top-flight management team led by CEO Hock Tan, have created a free-cash-flow machine that has delivered dividend increases of 10% annually.

Further, it's very important to understand that Broadcom is not taking on a massive debt load to invest in AI. In fact, it's just the opposite. As I reported in my widely read Seeking Alpha article, "Broadcom: What So Many Analysts And Investors Got Wrong – Buy The Dip," Broadcom actually reduced FY 2025 year-end long-term debt by $4.3 billion while total long-term liabilities were reduced by $8.2 billion. And that is exactly why I called out any "analysts" comparisons of Broadcom to Oracle as being irrational and irresponsible.

All that being the case, one would have thought recent announcements by two of Broadcom's biggest XPU, TPU, and networking customers – META and Google – to increase 2026 cap-ex spending to midpoints of $125 billion and $180 billion, respectively, would have significantly boosted AVGO shares. Yet, despite those cap-ex announcements being wildly bullish for Broadcom (at least in my opinion), the shares did not respond (at least not yet). In addition, Broadcom is one of Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) largest customers. Yet despite a report that TSMC's January revenue jumped a whopping 37% YoY, Broadcom's stock hardly budged.

The likely and latest reason for Broadcom stock's relatively weak performance despite the very bullish fundamental news flow is that Wall Street apparently has irrationally attached yet another false narrative to Broadcom: the previously mentioned "SaaSpocalypse." That's probably because Broadcom has intentionally grown its Infrastructure Software Segment with the recent strategic acquisition of VMware such that in FY 2025 the segment generated $27 billion in revenue (42% of total revenue), a gross margin of 93% (up two full percentage points YOY), and actually grew faster (26%) than the Semiconductor Segment (22%):

So, clearly, Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment is key to the company's success moving forward. There is no doubt about that. So, today, I will explain why the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative does not apply to Broadcom's software operations - at least not in the very negative way the market is apparently expecting.

AI Vibe-Coding

As you all know, all the leading AI large language models, or LLMs, have the ability to generate code. In my opinion, there is no doubt AI vibe-coding is going to be a huge productivity enhancer and that AI code generation is certainly going to harvest low-hanging fruit throughout the software sector.

For example, from its beginning, Anthropic has strategically focused on AI code generation. Earlier this month, Anthropic released new AI tools, in the form of Claude plugins, that were specifically tailored for the sales, finance, and legal professions. The announcement on 'X' caused panic selling across a number of related companies.

Last week privately held financial software provider Altruist Corp. launched an AI tool that creates tax strategies for clients. That caused a deep selloff in stocks like Charles Schwab Corp. (SCHW), Raymond James Financial (RJF), and Stifel Financial (SF).

So there is no doubt AI vibe-coding is disrupting the software sector – whether totally rational or not doesn't matter when the mere perception drives a stock down 8–10% in a day. But it is my opinion that some software companies are much more vulnerable than others. In my opinion, Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment has a wide moat and will continue to thrive. Here's why.

The Network Is The Computer

I have discussed this topic in some of my prior articles on Broadcom and highlighted the fact that as thousands of GPUs, XPUs, and TPUs are clustered together, the ability to keep them fed with the low-latency, high-bandwidth data required for these high-performance compute engines to function at full speed is absolutely critical to the overall success of a data center's ability to monetize AI.

Consider the following topology of a hypothetical data center architecture:

This slide was taken from a Broadcom Networking presentation on YouTube, which I encourage all investors to watch it.

This slide shows 4 data centers that, in a distributed computing architecture, need to communicate with each other and share data across the compute engines within and outside each data center. This is achieved through what the industry is calling a "Scale-Out Networking Interconnect." It is important to note that this is not a simple hardware interconnection problem – networking is also a very complex software-driven issue as well.

That is even more the case given that customers want to mix and match their choices of compute engines within each data center. Some companies need GPUs for LLM training as well as optimized XPUs for inference, and all companies would want second sources for compute engines for three reasons: to ensure access to enough compute capacity, to reduce costs, and to strengthen their negotiating positions with compute engine providers.

So, inside the data center diagram above, consider the following "Scale-Up" compute engine configuration (this slide, which was also taken from the previously referenced Broadcom presentation):

Note the three compute engines, A, B, and C, in the upper left-hand corner. "A" might be an Nvidia GPU, "B" might be a Broadcom XPU, and "C" might well be a Google TPU. All three compute engines need to (efficiently) run the full stack by the customer's chosen Infrastructure Software package. So, as you can see, not only are the data center interconnections heavily dependent on the networking technology, architecture, and Infrastructure Software, so too are the internal components of each leaf in the rack and each rack in the system.

Further, consider the following slide taken from a different Broadcom presentation given at the April 2024 OCP Regional Summit:

This slide refers to results from a study by META that shows just how important networking input/output is for AI recommendation workloads. What the results show is that networking was the bottleneck roughly 35% of the time. What this means is that those high-cost GPUs were idle 35% of the time because they were waiting on data, waiting to get rid of data, or on backups or error recovery processes.

One of the advantages of implementing Broadcom's networking equipment, and one reason Broadcom always seems to be one step ahead of the competition (if not two ...), is because of decades of experience. Broadcom arguably has the best networking development system on the planet – from both a hardware and software perspective. That is, Broadcom has a deep set of verification and validation tests it can run on any networking architecture to make sure the system functions properly and runs securely and with high performance.

Clearly then, networking optimization and infrastructure software is critical from both a functional and a performance perspective, right? And both are key to optimizing AI monetization.

Now, with that as background, pretend you are an IT executive at a Fortune 500 company that has decided to spend an estimated $4 billion on a 100,000 GPU cluster for an on-premises private cloud data center. The vast majority of those funds are going to be spent on expensive AI/ML GPUs, XPUs, and TPUs - with a few hundred million left over for networking equipment and infrastructure software – including required support for the following:

Now, ask yourself a simple question:

Should I risk my company's entire investment (and likely my job ...) on unproven AI vibe-coding solutions or should I simply stick with Broadcom's proven VMware Infrastructure Software solutions?

While the answer should be obvious, consider the following before answering: last June, Broadcom released VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) 9.0 that included several significant upgrades for private cloud operations:

  • Unified Operations and Experience.
  • Enhanced Performance and Efficiency.
  • Improved Networking and Security.

The enhanced Performance and Efficiency initiatives included (but weren't limited to) the following:

  1. "Advanced NVMe Memory Tiering: VCF 9.0 allows extending the memory pool with NVMe, treating fast NVMe devices as a lower-cost, second-tier memory. This frees up DRAM for active workloads, potentially leading to significant server consolidation by running more VMs or containers per host" and
  2. "New kernel optimizations can deliver up to 3x faster data speeds, lower latency, and reduced CPU overhead, especially beneficial for AI/ML workloads."

As you can see by these enhancements, VMware continues to make improvements to optimize networking performance. That's because Broadcom understands it's not just GPU performance, or networking performance, or software performance that matters - it's the overall system performance that counts. And Broadcom has been working for over a decade with partners like Google and META on optimizing the performance of every piece of the system.

International Data Corporation's ("IDC") conclusion about VCF 9.0:

Broadcom, through VMware, is strategically positioning VCF 9.0 to intensify competition within the hybrid cloud market, directly challenging hyperscalers by offering a unified, cost-optimized private/hybrid stack that addresses specific enterprise pain points. This move suggests a rebalancing of power in the cloud ecosystem, where the capabilities and economic advantages of modern private cloud solutions are gaining renewed prominence.

Risks

Now, could some of Broadcom's legacy mainframe and enterprise software solutions, like Symantec, be negatively impacted by AI vibe-coding? Perhaps ... but even that is not a given considering the large enterprise-level risks associated with making a huge change like that. But even if some revenue were to roll off those legacy software products as contracts expire, in the big picture, revenue growth in VMware AI Infrastructure Software sales will likely more than make up for them. And VCF is where the real action is going forward.

Summary & Conclusion

Due to the factors discussed above, Broadcom's VMware Enterprise Software solutions have a much wider moat as compared to the average everyday SaaS-based software company. That being the case, current concerns about AI vibe-coding taking a significant chunk out of Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment are, in my opinion, irrational.

Broadcom's guidance for Q1 FY 2026 was for revenue of ~$19.1 billion - an increase of 28% YoY. When the company releases its Q1 results after the market closes on March 4th, I expect they will be top and bottom line beats. That's not only because Broadcom has a history of conservative guidance that under-promises and over-delivers, but also because all the sector's news flow since last quarter has been very bullish.

As a result, and combined with recent and continued weakness in Broadcom's stock price despite very strong fundamental news from META, Google, and TSMC, I am upgrading Broadcom to a STRONG BUY.

data.title: Five9: Reset Valuation And New CEO Create Opportunity
data.description:
Five9, Inc. (FIVN) has given me the kind of setup I usually pay attention to. I upgraded it to a Buy in mid-October, and the stock is down nearly 30% since then. That drop does not automatically mean my call was wrong, but it definitely raises the standard for what I want to see next.

I think the timing is also perfect for a check-in. Five9 reports its Q4 and full-year FY25 results on February 19, 2026, so this is the window where we see expectations hardening and narratives start to form.

For me, though, the bigger wrinkle is leadership. Five9 appointed Amit Mathradas as CEO in December, and he stepped in on February 2, 2026. That CEO transition will affect how investors price uncertainty around the company, even if the underlying business stays the same.

First, though, I will review the company’s results from Q3 2025.

Q3 FY25 Results: Solid Execution And Capital Allocation

Before getting ahead of ourselves, it’s still worth grounding this discussion in what Five9 actually delivered in Q3.

Metric

Q3 FY25

$286M

+8%

Subscription revenue growth

—

+10%
